---
title: "oboro"
subtitle: "An anonymisation layer between your files and a language model."
---

You have a contract to reason about, and a model that could help.
The contract has a client's name in it, a bank account, a phone number.
Pasting it into Claude Code, Copilot, Codex or Cursor sends all of that to somebody else's computer.

`oboro` sits in between.
It reads the document, replaces the sensitive parts with stable placeholders, and keeps the mapping locally so the answer can be turned back into the real thing afterwards.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-flow
%%| fig-cap: "What happens to a document, and what never leaves the machine."

flowchart LR
    A[contract.docx] -->|oboro clean| B[contract.clean.md]
    B --> C{{paste into a model}}
    C --> D[the model's answer]
    D -->|oboro restore| E[answer with real values]
    A -.-> V[(encrypted vault)]
    V -.-> E

    style V stroke-dasharray: 4 4
```

The name is 朧, Japanese for hazy, or veiled by mist.
The tool blurs identifying detail rather than silencing it: the document still reads as a document, and the model can still reason about it.

## What it looks like

```text
Marie Lefevre a rencontre le directeur de Sogexia Partners hier.
Son numero est le 06 12 34 56 78.
```

becomes

```text
[[PERSON_1]] a rencontre le directeur de [[ORG_1]] hier.
Son numero est le [[PHONE_1]].
```

The same value always becomes the same placeholder, so a model can still tell that two documents mention the same client.

## What it reads

| Format | How |
| --- | --- |
| `.txt`, `.md` | Directly. |
| `.docx` | Text runs from the body, headers, footers, footnotes and comments. |
| `.xlsx`, `.xlsm` | Every sheet, flattened to rows. |
| `.pdf` | Embedded text. Scanned PDFs are refused rather than half-read. |
| `.png`, `.jpg`, `.tif` | Tesseract, in a build compiled with `--features ocr`. |

## What it finds

Structured values are matched by rules and confirmed by a real check, not just a regular expression.

| Kind | Confirmed by |
| --- | --- |
| Email addresses | Pattern. |
| Phone numbers | `libphonenumber`. |
| IBANs | ISO 13616 mod-97 checksum. |
| Payment cards | Luhn checksum, 13 to 19 digits. |
| SIRET | Luhn on both the SIREN prefix and the whole number. |
| SIREN | Luhn checksum. |
| IP addresses | Parsed as IPv4 or IPv6. |
| French addresses | Pattern. |
| Your own formats | Regular expressions you supply. |

Names, companies and addresses have no structure to match on.
Those are found by a recognition model that runs on your machine, described in [How it works](how-it-works.qmd).

::: {.callout-warning}

## Read the output before you send it

No tool of this kind catches everything, and this one deliberately errs towards redacting too much rather than too little.
Both failures matter, and neither is fixed by trusting the output unread.
[Limitations](limitations.qmd) is the honest account of where it falls short.

:::

## Where to go next

- [Quickstart](quickstart.qmd) to install it and clean your first document.
- [Reference](reference.qmd) for every command and option.
- [Confidentiality](confidentiality.qmd) for what is stored, where, and how.
